params.description: Vertical picture graph appears on left. Scale of 2. Each star symbol = 2.
Vertical picture graph appears on right. Scale of 5. Each star symbol = 5. Key highlighted.

beats.text: In Warmup, you saw something important. Same data, but the scale of 5 graph needed fewer symbols. That's because each symbol represents MORE.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph on left. Scale of 2. Same data as scale of 5 graph.
Vertical picture graph on right. Scale of 5. Key highlighted. Fewer symbols than scale of 2 graph.

beats.text: Same data, fewer symbols. That's why scale of 5 is useful when numbers get bigger. What does each symbol represent when the scale is 5?
What does each symbol represent when the scale is 5?

validator.description: Student selected 5, correct

beats.text: Each symbol represents 5 items. That's the scale.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph on left. Scale of 2.
Vertical picture graph on right. Scale of 5. Key highlighted. Multiple choice tool active.

params.description: Data table appears. Favorite Playground Activities. Swings: 20, Slides: 15, Monkey Bars: 25, Sandbox: 10. Vertical orientation.

beats.text: Let's see how 20 items become symbols when the scale is 5. Watch—we'll group by 5s.

elements.description: Data table showing Favorite Playground Activities. 20 items displayed for Swings, preparing to group by 5s. Key: Each ⭐ = 5.

beats.text: 5, 10, 15, 20. That's 4 groups of 5.

elements.description: Data table with Swings items grouped into 4 clusters of 5. Key: Each ⭐ = 5.

params.description: 4 groups transform into 4 symbols, stacking upward on picture graph for Swings column.

beats.text: 20 items equals 4 symbols. Each symbol shows 5.

params.description: Vertical picture graph appears. Swings column has 4 star symbols. Other columns empty. Key visible: Each star = 5.

beats.text: It is easier to read this picture graph if you can count by 5s. That's exactly what we do with scale of 5.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Swings column has 4 star symbols. Other columns empty. Key: Each star = 5.
Data table showing playground data alongside graph.

beats.text: Let's check: does the picture graph match our data table? 4 symbols, scale of 5. Count by 5s with me… 4 symbols, counting by 5s. What's the total?
What is the total represented by the picture graph?

validator.description: Student answered 20, correct

beats.text: 20 items. 5, 10, 15, 20. Skip counting tells you the total in the picture graph and that matches the value in the data table.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Swings column has 4 star symbols. Other columns empty. Key: Each star = 5.
Data table showing playground data alongside graph.

beats.text: You've read bar graphs before. Here's the same Playground data as a bar graph.

params.description: Picture graph transforms into vertical bar graph. Categories: Swings=20, Slides=15, Monkey Bars=25, Sandbox=10. Axis labeled 0–30 in 5s. All 4 bars pre-filled.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph. Playground Activities data. Swings=20, Slides=15, Monkey Bars=25, Sandbox=10. Axis labeled 0–30 in 5s.
Data table showing Playground Activities values.

beats.text: In a picture graph, you count by 5s using SYMBOLS: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25.

In a bar graph, you count by 5s using the numbers written along the side of the graph on the AXIS. Look—5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. Same skip-counting, just written on the side. Both graphs count by 5s. Where do you see the 5s on the bar graph?
Where do you see the 5s on the bar graph?

validator.description: Student selected On the axis, correct

beats.text: The axis shows the skip-counting: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. That's your scale of 5—built right into the side of the graph.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph. Playground Activities data. Swings=20, Slides=15, Monkey Bars=25, Sandbox=10. Axis labeled 0–30 in 5s.
Data table showing Playground Activities values.

params.description: Vertical picture graph appears. Favorite Playground Equipment data. Swings=4 symbols, Slides=3 symbols, Monkey bars=5 symbols. Key: each symbol = 5 votes.

Slides category highlights on picture graph.
Slides category highlights on bar graph.

beats.text: In a bar graph, you read the HEIGHT, where the bar ENDS on the axis. Look at Slides. What's the HEIGHT of the Slides bar? Where does it end?
What's the HEIGHT of the Slides bar?

validator.description: Student selected 15, correct bar height

beats.text: 15. The bar's HEIGHT is 15. It ends at 15 on the axis. Same as 3 symbols on the picture graph. Both show 15.

Axis numbers highlight to draw attention to the scale.

beats.text: Before you create the bars, look at the numbers on the axis.
These numbers count by 5s—just like you did with symbols. The axis does the skip-counting for you. What comes after 20 on this axis?
What comes after 20 on this axis?

validator.description: Student selected 25, correct

beats.text: 25. The axis counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30... Same skip-counting you used for symbols, just written on the side.

params.description: 7 symbols confirm in Liam row.

beats.text: 7 symbols for 35 books. 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35. That's how you count by the scale to place the right number of symbols.

params.description: Horizontal picture graph appears. Books Read This Month data. Liam=7 symbols (35 books), Emma=4 symbols (20 books), Noah=5 symbols (25 books), Olivia=8 symbols (40 books). Key visible: each symbol = 5 books.

beats.text: Use your graph to find how many fewer books Emma read than Olivia.
How many FEWER books did Emma read compared to Olivia?

validator.description: Student selected 20, correct difference between Olivia and Emma

beats.text: 20 fewer. Olivia read 40. Emma read 20. The difference is 20.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph in reading mode. Books Read This Month data. Liam=7 symbols (35), Emma=4 symbols (20), Noah=5 symbols (25), Olivia=8 symbols (40). Key visible.

params.description: Horizontal picture graph appears. Books Read data. Liam=35 (7 symbols), Emma=20 (4 symbols), Noah=25 (5 symbols), Olivia=40 (8 symbols). Key: Each star = 5 books. Scale toggle control visible with three options: Each star = 1, Each star = 2, Each star = 5. Currently set to scale of 5.
Data table appears alongside graph showing Books Read values: Liam=35, Emma=20, Noah=25, Olivia=40.

beats.text: You built this graph with scale of 5. But what would it look like with a different scale? Try it.
Toggle the scale to 1. What happens?

validator.description: Student toggled scale from 5 to 1

params.description: Symbols multiply as scale changes to 1. Olivia: 8 symbols → 40 symbols. Liam: 7 symbols → 35 symbols. Noah: 5 symbols → 25 symbols. Emma: 4 symbols → 20 symbols. Key updates to Each star = 1 book.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph. Books Read data at scale of 1. Liam=35 (35 symbols), Emma=20 (20 symbols), Noah=25 (25 symbols), Olivia=40 (40 symbols). Key: Each star = 1 book. Scale toggle control active.
Data table showing Books Read values: Liam=35, Emma=20, Noah=25, Olivia=40.

beats.text: Olivia went from 8 symbols to 40. That's a lot of symbols. Now try scale of 2.
Toggle the scale to 2.

validator.description: Student toggled scale from 1 to 2

params.description: Symbols reduce as scale changes to 2. Olivia: 40 symbols → 20 symbols. Liam: 35 symbols → 17.5 symbols (half-symbol appears). Noah: 25 symbols → 12.5 symbols (half-symbol appears). Emma: 20 symbols → 10 symbols. Key updates to Each star = 2 books.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph. Books Read data at scale of 2. Liam=35 (17.5 symbols), Emma=20 (10 symbols), Noah=25 (12.5 symbols), Olivia=40 (20 symbols). Half-symbols visible for odd values. Key: Each star = 2 books. Scale toggle control active.
Data table showing Books Read values: Liam=35, Emma=20, Noah=25, Olivia=40.

beats.text: Fewer symbols, but still more than scale of 5. Toggle back to 5.
Toggle the scale back to 5.

validator.description: Student toggled scale from 2 to 5

params.description: Graph returns to original state. Olivia: 20 symbols → 8 symbols. Liam: 17.5 symbols → 7 symbols. Noah: 12.5 symbols → 5 symbols. Emma: 10 symbols → 4 symbols. Key updates to Each star = 5 books.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph. Books Read data at scale of 5. Liam=35 (7 symbols), Emma=20 (4 symbols), Noah=25 (5 symbols), Olivia=40 (8 symbols). Key: Each star = 5 books. Scale toggle control active.
Data table showing Books Read values: Liam=35, Emma=20, Noah=25, Olivia=40.

beats.text: Same 40 books for Olivia every time. Scale of 1: 40 symbols. Scale of 2: 20 symbols. Scale of 5: just 8.
Which scale uses the fewest symbols?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 5, correct

beats.text: Scale of 5. Bigger scale, fewer symbols, same data.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph. Books Read data at scale of 5. Liam=35 (7 symbols), Emma=20 (4 symbols), Noah=25 (5 symbols), Olivia=40 (8 symbols). Key: Each star = 5 books. Scale toggle control inactive after MC question answered.
Data table showing Books Read values: Liam=35, Emma=20, Noah=25, Olivia=40.

beats.text: All three showed the same information. The scale just changes how many symbols you need. Now let's use your graph to answer some questions.

beats.text: You made both kinds of graphs today. Here's one last question. Picture graphs use symbols. Bar graphs use bars. What do these graphs have in common?
What do these graphs have in common?
options: They both use the same scale

condition.selected: They both use the same scale

validator.description: Student selected 'They both use the same scale', correct

beats.text: Both use scale of 5. Picture graphs show it with symbols—count by 5s. Bar graphs show it on the side. Same scale, different format.

Horizontal bar graph. Screen Time data. Scale of 5 shown on vertical axis.
